content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 21, 1995

Nancy F. Lea
15130 Road 99
Woodland, CA 95695

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-096

Dear Ms. Lea:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as a member of the Yolo County Planning Commission under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

As a member of the Yolo County Planning Commissioner, does the Act prohibit you from interacting with the Yolo County Planning Commission or the Yolo County Board of Supervisors with respect to your own real property interests?

CONCLUSION

As a member of the Yolo County Planning Commission, you may not make, participate in making or influence any governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property. However, you may appear before the Yolo County Planning Commission in the course of its prescribed governmental function to represent your personal

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-096
Page 2


interests in real property so long as you appear in the same
manner as any other member of the general public.

In addition, you may contact the Yolo County Board of
Supervisors so long as you do not act or purport to act on behalf
of, or as the representative of, the Yolo County Planning
Commission in contacting any member, officer, employee or
consultant of the board of supervisors.

Finally, the Act does not restrict your ability to
communicate with the general public or press.

FACTS

You are a member of the Yolo County Planning Commission. A
request has been filed with the planning commission by the Wilbur-
Ellis Company to split a 43 acre parcel of land and to obtain a
conditional use permit to operate a fertilizer/farm chemical
company on the site.

You and your spouse own 160 acres adjacent to the property
that is the subject of this decision. You operate a walnut ranch
on the property and have built your residence on your property.
You have asked whether you may participate in your private
capacity in activities pertaining to this decision.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts of Interest, Generally

As we discussed on March 13, 1995, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making
or in any way attempting to use the official's position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest. As a member of
the Yolo County Planning Commission, you are a "public official"
as defined in the Act. (Section 82048; Section 87200.)

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable²
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any
economic interest of the official, including:

2
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. A-95-096
Page 3


     (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                              Section 87103(b).

     It is foreseeable that the fertilizer plant in proximity to
your property will have a financial effect on the value of your
property.  Moreover, since the subject property is adjacent to
your property, pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(a)(1), the effect is
deemed to be material.3  Thus, you may not make, participate in
making, or influence the decisions regarding the Wilbur-Ellis
property.

     The conflict-of-interest prohibition has been interpreted
broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.  Thus, where a
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your property, you may not vote on the
decision or make contacts with, appear before, or otherwise
attempt to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant
of your agency concerning the governmental decision. (Regulations
18700 and 18700.1.)  "Influencing" includes contacts with and
appearances before any member, officer, employee or consultant of
the agency.

Exceptions

     However, Regulation 18700.1 expressly provides a series of
limited exceptions:

     1. First, Regulation 18700.1 permits an otherwise
disqualified official to appear in the same manner as any other
member of the general public before the official's own agency in
the course of its prescribed governmental function to represent
his or her personal interests in real property.  Since you and
your spouse wholly own your ranch, you may appear before the Yolo
County Planning Commission in public meetings so long as you
appear in the same manner as any other member of the public to
advocate on behalf of your property.

     This would include signing a petition in your private
capacity, in the same manner as the general public, as a means of
representing your wholly owned property interests.  However, this

___________________________
3 You noted in our telephone conversation that the fertilizer
plant is actually proposed for a more distant portion of the
Wilbur-Ellis property.  As we discussed, there may be a point in
time when the plant site can be analyzed independently.  This may
result in a different materiality standard applying.  At this
time, however, the decisions concern the Wilbur-Ellis property
adjacent to your ranch as a whole.

Our File No. A-95-096
Page 4


exception would not encompass circulating or contacting your
agency to deliver the petition since the petition embodies the
interest of other signatories as well.

Please note, however, that this exception is limited to your
personal interests and your comments should be so limited.
(Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151.) Thus, you may not appear
before your own agency to represent any other person's interests
or any group of citizens. You should take care to clarify that
you are not acting in any official capacity.

2. In addition, pursuant to Regulation 18700.1(c), an
official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision where the official contacts members of other
agencies (not appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of
the official's agency). This is the case provided that the
official does not act or purport to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her own agency.⁴

3. Finally, Regulation 18700.1(b)(2) provides that where a
public official communicates with the general public or the press,
the official is not attending to use his or her official position
to influence a governmental decision. Thus, you may communicate
with constituents regarding the issue.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁵

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
    Counsel, Legal Division


_________________________

⁴ Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of
official stationery.

⁵ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
